Exclusive Investigation · UK Companies House · Insolvency Service Ref: CC-2023-115641

Jonathan Willis:
The Fall of Blacks Club,
the Crypto Millions
& a 25-Year Trail of
Broken Promises

A multi-year investigation into how Jonathan Willis — director of PMCC Ltd (No. 12188280) — allegedly misled investors across 25 years, routing funds through a network of 31 connected UK entities, and systematically offering free shares in new ventures as compensation each time a prior investment collapsed. From luxury London private members' clubs and film finance, to crypto tokens, NFT life memberships, Spanish property developments and now a new wellness ecosystem — the ventures change, the pattern does not: the money comes in, the promises dissolve, and a new proposition is already being offered before the last one has been accounted for.

Across these 31 companies, 816 shareholder entries have been recorded — representing 497 unique individuals who have, at one time or another, held equity in a Willis-connected entity. Confirmed losses from documented cases run to hundreds of thousands of pounds; creditor claims in the ONIB liquidation alone total £1,232,113 — for a single unfinished film. Across the full network and all investment cycles, the cumulative total is estimated to run into the millions. Central to this investigation: the director's own admission that remuneration recorded in company accounts as a Director's Loan was in fact his salary — a mischaracterisation that goes to the heart of how investor funds were used and reported.

LIQUI­DATION

⚑ Critical Alert · Asset Distancing Pattern

The Overseas Transfer — A Coordinated Exit

On 4 March 2024 — a single day — Jonathan Willis resigned as director from five UK companies simultaneously and relinquished Person with Significant Control (PSC) status in three of them. This was not a gradual wind-down. It was a coordinated, single-day corporate exit spanning the full breadth of his active network.

On the same day, control of three entities was transferred to the individual described as Edson Kajihara (alleged Brazilian national, Brazil resident) — using a shared London registered office address (Lancaster Court, 36–39 Newman Street, W1T 1QH) that had been used across multiple Willis companies for years. No explanation was provided to shareholders. His identity has not been independently verified.

The timing is significant. The Insolvency Service complaint had been filed in November 2023. PMCC Ltd was in liquidation. The transfer of control to an overseas party using a serviced office address — rather than a genuine operational handover — raises serious questions about whether the purpose was to distance UK assets and company control from creditors and the ongoing investigation.

Companies resigned — 4 March 2024

→ Dune Drifter Ltd (11995323) → Kung Fu Princess Ltd (10020691) → Fistful of Lead Ltd (10665548) → One Night in Bath Ltd (10427464) → M.A.S.I. Ltd (03403434)

PSC transferred same day to

The individual described as Edson Kajihara

Alleged Brazilian national · alleged Brazil resident
Identity not independently verified
Correspondence: Lancaster Court, London W1T 1QH
(same shared office used across Willis network)
Received PSC in: Dune Drifter, Fistful of Lead, One Night in Bath

Corroborating context

On 1 March 2024 — three days before the resignations — the director sent an investor a WhatsApp message from a hospital. On 5 March, he reported still being "really bad." Companies House filings confirm five director resignations were nevertheless processed in this window. By June 2025 he was confirmed to have emigrated to Dubai.

Section 01 · Photo Evidence

Dubai, May 2024
While the Liquidation Continued

These photographs were obtained by the activist investor group and shared internally in December 2025. They place the subject networking at social and investment events in Dubai in May 2024 — six months after the Insolvency Service complaint was filed and while the PMCC liquidation investigation was ongoing. The TOKEN2049 conference photograph is particularly significant: it places him at one of the world's largest crypto investor conferences, consistent with reports of continued fundraising activity under his LinkedIn profile describing himself as an "RWA Tokenisation Consultant." Faces of all individuals other than the subject have been obscured.

⚑ Significance of these photographs

These photographs confirm the subject's presence in Dubai at investment and crypto networking events in May 2024. At this point: PMCC Ltd was in formal liquidation; the Insolvency Service complaint had been filed six months prior; five director resignations had been filed just two months earlier (4 March 2024); and multiple companies were pending strike-off. His LinkedIn simultaneously described him as an "RWA Tokenisation Consultant, Originator" — entirely consistent with seeking new investment while the UK investigation remained unresolved and creditors unpaid.

Property Evidence

Llandrinio Hall
The Companies House Address

Llandrinio Hall (Llandrinio, Llanymynech, Powys, SY22 6SG) is a Grade II listed property dating to 1682, described as the oldest brick building in Montgomeryshire. It was used as the registered Companies House address for Posterity Entertainment Ltd, Ruon Marketing Ltd, and Battle Island Ltd. The property was marketed for sale through Savills in 2023 (£1.35m) and relisted in March 2024 at £1.1m. A source with direct knowledge confirms the subject and his wife were marketing the property while he was operating Blacks. The address was identified as a key link in December 2025 — connecting the "Peter Parfait" alias (Ruon Marketing Ltd) directly to the subject's corporate network.

Section 03 · Scale of Impact

The Scale of
Investor Losses

A cross-reference of UK Companies House filings and the activist group's investor spreadsheet reveals the full reach of the corporate network. Across 31 identified entities, 816 shareholder entries have been recorded — representing 497 unique named individuals who have, at some point over a 25-year period, held equity in a company connected to Jonathan Willis. These are not institutional investors. They are private individuals who trusted prospectuses, newsletters, and personal assurances.

816
Total Shareholder
Entries Recorded
Across all 31 companies
Sources: CH + investor spreadsheet

Unique Individuals
Identified by Name
Private investors, not
institutional shareholders
31
Connected Companies
Verified CH Mar 2026
Spanning 25 years
across multiple sectors
Millions
estimated
Full Network
(see below)
Activist group losses: £100,000s confirmed
ONIB creditor claims alone: £1,232,113
Full network: millions estimated

What the 816 figure means

The 816 total represents individual shareholder entries across all 31 companies — meaning one person holding shares in three different Willis companies counts as three entries. This figure reflects the breadth of the network: the same pool of individuals was repeatedly recruited into successive ventures, with each new company adding further entries to the register. The 816 entries are concentrated in a handful of companies — Kung Fu Princess (135), KA Film (120), Bath Attractions (118), and Posterity Entertainment (104) together account for over half the total.

What the 497 figure means

497 unique named individuals have been identified across the full network — but this is almost certainly an undercount. Many companies have CH-filed shareholder counts that significantly exceed the named entries in the investor spreadsheet: PMCC Ltd alone has 67 shareholders on record at Companies House while the spreadsheet carries only one entry. Ioptec Ltd has 42 CH-registered shareholders; Doris Spinning Company has 24. The true number of individuals affected across all 31 companies and 25 years of activity is likely considerably higher than 497.

Why confirmed losses could run into millions

The documented losses from the activist group alone — a subset of investors who organised around PMCC — include one family's £42,500, individual losses of £23,000 and £20,000+, and numerous others whose amounts were not disclosed. This group represents a fraction of the full 497 unique individuals identified. Companies with large shareholder registers — Posterity Entertainment (104 shareholders, £200k capital), One Night in Bath (76 shareholders), Special Situations Syndicate (51–62 shareholders), and RUON Red (52 shareholders) — collectively represent hundreds of additional investors whose losses are not captured in the activist group's evidence. The scale is now anchored by a hard figure from a court-filed document: the ONIB liquidator's progress report (December 2025) records creditor claims totalling £1,232,113.04 in that company alone — for a film that was never finished. This is a single company, one of at least four in formal insolvency across the network. Across 25 years, four distinct investment cycles, and 31 companies, the cumulative total of investor losses across the full network is estimated to run into the millions of pounds.

Section 04 · Central Finding

The Cycle of
Recruitment & Loss

Across 25 years and 31 companies, a consistent sequence emerges. Each time an investment vehicle collapses or fails to return funds, investors are not repaid in cash. Instead, they are offered equity stakes or shares in a new venture — framing loss as opportunity and sustaining engagement through forward-looking promises rather than backward-looking accountability. The 816 shareholder entries and 497 unique individuals identified across the network are, in significant part, the product of this cycle: the same pool of people recruited again and again into successive vehicles. The following five-step sequence has been documented repeating across at least four distinct investment generations.

01

A new venture is launched with compelling narrative

A new company or investment opportunity is created, positioned around a timely theme — property investment, independent film, crypto/NFT membership clubs, metaverse, tokenisation. An Information Memorandum, prospectus, or newsletter is circulated. Urgency is created: closing rounds, limited availability, "only a few held back." The opportunity is presented as either already profitable or on the cusp of breakthrough.

Property Auction News (c.2008) RUON Tokens (2019–20) Crypto Club Global NFTs (2021) PMCC SEIS (2022) Dubai Club Membership (2023) Mickey Meme Coin (2024) House of Frankenstein shares (2025)
02

Investors commit funds — financial reality is concealed

Investors are recruited, often from a pool already engaged with prior ventures. Key financial information is withheld or misrepresented. In the PMCC case, investors were explicitly told the underlying business was breaking even and financially stable. Costs that doubled revenues and an inherited debt of over £270,000 were not disclosed. SEIS tax relief eligibility is used to reduce apparent investment risk — the upfront income tax rebate makes the net cost of investing feel lower, while also creating practical barriers to exit if things go wrong.

"I asked very clearly about the financial situation and was told it was breaking even. I specifically said I didn't want my investment to cover any black hole in the books." — Investor, October 2023
03

The venture fails — funds cannot be returned

The venture collapses, enters insolvency, or simply ceases to deliver returns. In multiple cases, investors requesting return of funds are told the money has "already been spent." In one documented case, an investor who requested a refund within days of investing — before any plausible deployment of their funds — was told recovery was impossible. Directors resign at strategic moments, often within days of corporate collapses, to limit personal liability.

"The answer was a resounding 'no', and [he] explained that it had already been spent. I asked for more info but he was quite condescending — 'oh you know, staff need to get paid.'" — Investor, on requesting PMCC refund days after investing
04

Free shares or equity swaps are offered in lieu of repayment

Rather than returning investor funds, the director offers shares, tokens, or equity stakes in a new or adjacent venture as compensation. This offer is typically framed as generous — investors are positioned to benefit from "the next opportunity" rather than lose money. In practice, the new vehicle then follows the same trajectory. Documented examples span: RUON tokens → KA Film shares → Posterity shares; Let's Be Evil shares → Kung Fu Princess shares → Posterity shares; outstanding loans → Spain development equity; outstanding loans → NFT memberships; outstanding loans → Mickey meme coin allocation.

"He had offered to swap or exchange the debt for other projects such as the project in Spain or the Mickey meme coin." — Investor, June 2025
"My shares in the loss-making film project Let's Be Evil have been converted into shares of Kung Fu Princess. The way things are going, Kung Fu Princess too will become loss-making, and transfer to Posterity… so you see why I said JW's projects are a spider's web." — Long-term investor (dealings since 2006)
05

A new venture is launched — the cycle restarts

With existing investors now holding equity in the new vehicle, a fresh round of investment is sought — either from the same pool (now emotionally committed) or from new investors unaware of the prior history. The stated holding company, Posterity Entertainment Ltd, was explicitly described as a vehicle to "sweep up lossmakers by a profit-making company, so that shareholders would not lose out." In practice no lossmaker appears to have been rescued by Posterity. Instead, Posterity itself is now facing an active proposal to strike off, with accounts overdue since October 2024. As of late 2025, new investment is reportedly being sought through "House of Frankenstein" shares and tokenisation projects — consistent with step one of a new cycle.

Cycle 1 · Property newsletters (c.2003–2011) Cycle 2 · Film companies (2014–2023) Cycle 3 · Crypto/NFT clubs (2019–2023) Cycle 4 · Tokenisation / HoF shares (2024–present)

Section 05

Key
Findings

Critical Finding · Financial Misrepresentation

Director's Salary Allegedly Misrecorded as Director's Loan in Filed Accounts

Investors and one financial professional within the group contend this is not a matter of accounting error or ambiguity. During a call with liquidators TMP, the director himself stated explicitly that the remuneration he drew from the company was his salary for services as director. Yet the same funds are recorded in PMCC Ltd's filed accounts as a Director's Loan Account — a fundamentally different classification with different legal and tax implications.

A Director's Loan represents a debt owed back to the company — money that a director is obligated to repay. A salary is compensation for services rendered. Recording a salary as a loan obscures the true nature of director remuneration from shareholders and creditors. It means the full cost of paying the director was not visible in the accounts as a trading expense. It also means that the £116,000 written off in the TMP liquidation documents — recorded as a director's loan — may represent salary payments that were never put through payroll, never subject to PAYE, and never reported to HMRC in the normal way.

One investor with a financial background observed that the director's stated rationale — that fees were taken as a "self-employed individual" rather than an employee — is unlikely to withstand IR35 and PAYE scrutiny given the nature and duration of his sole directorship. A shareholder who questioned this in the liquidator meeting received no satisfactory explanation. The director was also unable to recall basic financial details including the company's own bank account numbers — described by multiple investors as extraordinary for a sole director of numerous active companies.

"Documentation suggests Jonathan Willis reported to the liquidator that he received a director's salary but labelled it internally as a director's loan. JW stated in that call that they were taking fees not as directors but as 'self-employed individuals', which is the reason it seems they were left as a loan. I find this a ridiculous argument and not one that would stand up to IR35/PAYE scrutiny." — Investor with financial background, activist group
"There is 116k that has been written off. This appears to be funds that JW took from the company as 'fees' which are unjustified and excessive given the financial state of affairs. It is proper accounting practice to show the purpose/reason for any DLA withdrawal." — Investor, PMCC Issues & Concerns document

Investors argue the significance of this cannot be overstated. PMCC's accounts filed at Companies House present a materially different picture of how funds were used than the director's own verbal account to the liquidator. The director has confirmed the discrepancy himself. Investors contend this represents a misrepresentation of how investor funds were deployed, embedded in official filed accounts — a matter the Insolvency Service investigation is expected to address.

Finding 02 · Misrepresentation at Point of Investment

Investors Explicitly Told the Business Was Breaking Even

Multiple investors state they were told by both directors that Blacks Ltd was breaking even and in a better financial position than when acquired. Investors specifically asked whether funds would cover existing liabilities; they were assured they would not. The true position — costs of £20,000 per week against revenue of £10,000, plus £270,000+ of inherited debt — was disclosed only in the director's October 2023 letter, after the business had already collapsed. The director's own letter contradicts the representations made at the point of investment.

"When I was in discussions regarding investment, this information was not forthcoming. I went further to add I didn't want my investment to cover any black hole in the books — and this has clearly been the case." — Investor, October 2023

Finding 03 · Free Shares as Debt Instrument — Systematic Pattern

Losses Repeatedly Converted Into Equity in New Ventures Rather Than Repaid

Across at least three documented investment cycles, debts owed to investors have been offered to be converted into shares or tokens in new ventures rather than repaid in cash. In each case the new venture ultimately also fails to return value. The chain is documented: RUON tokens → Posterity shares; KA Film debt → Posterity shares; Let's Be Evil shares → Kung Fu Princess shares; outstanding loans → Spain development equity; outstanding loans → Mickey meme coin; outstanding loans → NFT memberships. One long-term investor (dealings since 2006) describes this explicitly as a mechanism by which one failed venture feeds the next, with a new offering launched just before the prior one is exposed as failing — reducing investor scepticism.

"A new, exciting-sounding project is launched just before the film release or fruition of another project, so one is less likely to be sceptical of the outcome. Films can be slow burners, so the film shareholders have mainly been waiting and hoping — or didn't ask many questions, as the character Max Bialystock was hoping in The Producers." — Long-term investor, 2024

Finding 04 · Undisclosed Loan to Director-Controlled Film Company

PMCC Funds Loaned to KA Film Ltd — Sole Director: Willis — Without Investor Disclosure

A significant undisclosed loan was made from PMCC Ltd to KA Film Ltd (Company No. 11120322), of which the director was the sole director. No charge was registered. The loan was not disclosed to investors as a potential use of funds. The director resigned from KA Film on 24 October 2023, leaving no one running a company that owed money to PMCC — a company that was itself already in financial distress. KA Film is currently listed on Companies House as Active — Active proposal to strike off, with accounts overdue since December 2022.

"An unsecured loan to a film company was not only irresponsible, a clear conflict of interests by the director who failed to understand his fiduciary duties, but probably also ultra vires. At what point were we trading so profitably that the director considered we had money to burn?" — Investor with financial background

Finding 04b · UK Future Fund / Covid Loans — One Night in Bath Ltd · Liquidator's Progress Report December 2025 · Document Verified

ONIB: Government Loans, Bladud Debt, Willis Bankruptcy, and Ongoing Investigation — All Now Document-Confirmed

Companies House confirms that One Night in Bath Ltd (10427464) was placed into compulsory liquidation by court order (Manchester District Registry, No. 908 of 2024). Winding-up order made 27 August 2024; liquidator Stephen Hunt (Griffins, IP number 9183) appointed 11 October 2024. A 35-page progress report was filed 17 December 2025 (WU07), covering the period 11 October 2024 to 10 October 2025. The petitioner was UK FF Nominees Limited (the UK Future Fund). A virtual creditors' meeting was held via Microsoft Teams on 9 January 2026 at 10:00am to approve the liquidator's remuneration — some investors in the activist group were unaware of this meeting.

£500,000 UKFF Convertible Loan — basis of winding-up petition: The liquidator confirms ONIB entered into a Convertible Loan Agreement with UKFF on 23 March 2021 for £500,000, guaranteed by the UK Government under the Future Fund scheme. The loan reached maturity on 23 March 2024 (36 months). No payments were ever made. The winding-up petition was presented on 8 July 2024 and the order made 27 August 2024.

£50,000 Bounce Back Loan — Metro Bank, June 2020 — liquidator states company appears ineligible: Initial investigations identified a Bounce Back Loan of £50,000 from Metro Bank taken in June 2020. The liquidator reviewed the loan documentation and bank statements, made further enquiries with the directors, and states: "Following review of the available documentation, the Company does not seem to have been entitled to apply for and receive the loan." The liquidator has not provided further detail at this stage as disclosure may prejudice future action. No determination yet as to whether recovery will benefit creditors.

£126,409.68 Bladud Spring loan — connected by common directorship — enforcement ongoing: On 5 April 2017, ONIB entered into a loan agreement with Bladud Spring Limited, described in the report as "a connected company by way of common directorship." The sum advanced was £126,409.68. The loan remains outstanding. The liquidator has demanded repayment; an advisor acting for Bladud has responded stating the company is unable to repay and that enforcement attempts will result in Bladud's own liquidation. Bladud is described as balance sheet insolvent. The liquidator is pursuing enforcement and this matter forms part of the ongoing investigation. Whether the Bladud loan was funded from UKFF proceeds — which under the CLA terms were restricted to working capital — is a question the investigation may address.

Willis bankruptcy — liquidator has submitted a claim against Willis, not the reverse: The report confirms that Jonathan Willis was made bankrupt on 15 January 2025. The liquidator has been liaising with Willis's Trustee in Bankruptcy and has submitted a claim in Willis's bankruptcy estate in respect of monies owed by Willis to the Company. This is the opposite of what was previously stated in an earlier version of this report — Willis is the debtor here, not a claimant against ONIB.

Edson Kajihara — transfer investigated and closed — interview confirmed: Section 4.3.3 of the report states: "On 4 March 2024, Mr Willis resigned as a director of the Company and transferred his shareholding to Edson Kajihara ('Mr Kajihara'), who was appointed as the sole director of the Company on the same date. I have made enquiries of the directors and interviewed Mr Kajihara to fully investigate the circumstances regarding the transfer. I have concluded my investigations into the transfer of the Company, as there is no prospect that this area of investigation will bring any financial benefit to creditors." This confirms: (a) Kajihara is a real, identifiable person who was interviewed; (b) the liquidator investigated the transfer and has formally closed that line of enquiry; (c) no financial claim arising from the transfer is being pursued.

Ongoing bank account investigations — Coutts and Metro Bank: The company held accounts with Coutts and Metro Bank. The liquidator has obtained statements and identified transactions requiring further investigation. Various third parties have responded to enquiries. The investigations remain ongoing. Prism 339 Ltd was engaged on a fixed fee of £600 plus VAT to convert bank statements into a format suitable for review — a data conversion task rather than an ongoing forensic retainer.

Creditor position: Total creditors per the Official Receiver's report: £1,176,879.50. Claims submitted to the liquidator total £1,232,113.04, not yet adjudicated. HMRC is understood to have a VAT claim of £3,013.32. No dividend prospects can be estimated at present. Liquidator's fees incurred to date: £99,769.50 (241.83 hours). Fee cap proposed: £175,250.

Production switch rationale: The report records that "due to the pandemic, the Company switched projects to an interactive film (which were performing well during this time) which was called 'Mia and the Dragon Princess'." This is Willis's explanation as recorded by the liquidator. Investors dispute this rationale: a source with direct knowledge reports appearing as an extra on both productions and that ONIB filming was still ongoing as late as summer 2023, suggesting the productions were not mutually exclusive. The source also reports that Covid disclaimers were required for access to the Mia/KFP set, consistent with filming during restrictions. The pandemic justification does not address why ONIB's government loans were not used to continue ONIB production, or why £126,409.68 was lent to a connected company.

CH Verified · Insolvency tab · 10427464 · March 2026

Finding 05 · Zero Crypto Assets Declared Despite Operating as Crypto Club

PMCC Collected ETH Payments — Liquidator Declares Zero Crypto Assets

PMCC Ltd positioned itself as London's first crypto/metaverse private members' club, soliciting investment and membership payments in Ethereum. Investors were offered NFT memberships and SEIS equity. Despite this, the liquidator's records show zero crypto assets declared. Investors have formally requested a complete list of all Ethereum and Bitcoin wallet addresses used by PMCC, plus full blockchain transaction history to the point of conversion to fiat. This request has been submitted to TMP. The concern: crypto wallet anonymity makes it straightforward to conceal assets that fiat bank statements would not reveal.

Finding 06 · Posterity Digital Investment Club — Funds Collected, Club Never Launched

Written Confirmation: PDIC Was Never Launched — Funds Used Elsewhere

Investors paid into the Posterity Digital Investment Club in October 2021 via a dedicated bank account (sort code 23-05-80, account ████████). The club was never launched. Written confirmation of this has been provided to at least one investor by both co-director Adam Leon and the director's wife. The funds were used elsewhere. At least one investor is pursuing a direct fraud claim on this basis.

"I have written confirmation of this from both Adam Leon and Polina Willis. I am pursuing [him] directly for the return of these funds as they were clearly misappropriated." — Investor, activist group

Finding 07 · The Brazilian Connection — Mass Exit on One Day

Five Simultaneous Resignations — Control Transferred to Overseas National, Same Date

On 4 March 2024, the director filed five director resignations simultaneously. Person with Significant Control (PSC) status in three entities was transferred the same day to the individual described as Edson Kajihara (alleged Brazilian national, Brazil resident) using the shared Lancaster Court, Newman Street registered office address. On 1 March 2024, the director sent an investor a WhatsApp message from a hospital. On 5 March he reported still being "really bad." Companies House records show five director resignations were nevertheless filed during this same period. The investor group describes the coordinated transfer as designed to distance assets from UK creditors.

Finding 08 · Systematic Failure to Notify Shareholders — Statutory Breach

No AGMs Held, No Shareholder Notices Issued — Compounded by EIS/SEIS Practical Constraints

Across multiple entities, shareholders were never notified of Annual General Meetings, financial difficulties, or impending insolvency — a statutory obligation under UK company law. This is not merely a matter of poor communication: failure to notify shareholders of material events is a legal breach. The problem is compounded by the EIS and SEIS investment structures used to raise funds. Selling EIS or SEIS shares within three years of the company commencing trading does not legally prohibit a sale — but it immediately triggers clawback of the income tax relief claimed, potential backdating of a prior year's tax code, and the kind of HMRC interaction that can itself attract scrutiny of the investor's wider tax affairs. For shares in an unlisted company where no market exists and no buyer can realistically be found, investors face a situation that is functionally equivalent to being locked in — even though no legal restriction formally applies. The result is a captive shareholder base: no information provided, no viable exit available, and a tax structure that strongly disincentivises any attempt to cut losses once concerns arise.

A concrete illustration of the timescales involved: one investor in One Night in Bath Ltd was recruited in 2015. Filming did not commence until 2023. The company entered liquidation in 2026. That is an eleven-year span during which investors received no formal update, no AGM notice, and no meaningful opportunity to exit. This is not exceptional — it is the documented pattern across the network.

"The EIS/SEIS lock-in structure means investors are trapped. You cannot sell, you cannot exit, and if you're not told the company is failing, you have no ability to take any action at all." — Investor, activist group

Note: this investor's characterisation reflects the practical reality — exits are technically possible but carry tax consequences significant enough that they are unlikely in almost all circumstances for shares of this kind.

Finding 09 · Regulatory Escalation

Investor Group Plans Coordinated Escalation to FCA, NCA and Report Fraud

The activist group's December 2025 formal report documents concerns about the pace and visibility of regulatory responses to date. The group notes that reporting authorities and Companies House have not, to their knowledge, initiated visible action in response to the concerns raised. The liquidation of PMCC Ltd is an ongoing process; investors report that substantive updates from the liquidators have been limited, which they attribute to the nature and complexity of the insolvency process rather than any failing specific to those appointed. The group plans coordinated escalation to the FCA, NCA, and Report Fraud collectively in early 2026. Their stated objective: permanent director disqualification and a formal fraud investigation. Note: Action Fraud was replaced by Report Fraud (reportfraud.police.uk) in January 2026.

13799175 MARCHES ALTERNATIVE CONSTRUCTION LTD Active — Conf. overdue 14 Dec 2021 Resigned Oct 2023 San Marco House, 26 Craig Yr Eos Road, Ogmore-By-Sea, Bridgend, CF32 0PG 41202 JW resigned Oct 2023. Accounts filed to Dec 2024 but confirmation statement overdue Dec 2025. 25–50% JW shareholding. Welsh address.
13143833 RUON MARKETING LTD Dissolved 27 Dec 2022 19 Jan 2021 Dissolved Llandrinio Hall, Llandrinio, Llanymynech, Powys, SY22 6SG 96090 Dissolved 27 December 2022. Llandrinio Hall address — confirms property connection. Also linked to co-director Adam Leon. Investor paid £500 for RUON tokens, told "all lost in Ukraine."
12816801 BATTLE ISLAND LTD Dissolved 16 Apr 2024 17 Aug 2020 Dissolved Llandrinio Hall, Llandrinio, Llanymynech, SY22 6SG 59111 Dissolved 16 April 2024. Registered at Llandrinio Hall — confirms address connection. Majority shareholding via Posterity.
12390135 POSTERITY ENTERTAINMENT LTD Active — Strike-off Proposed 07 Jan 2020 Resigned Jan 2025 Llandrinio Hall, Llandrinio, Llanymynech, Powys, SY22 6SG
↳ Llandrinio Hall address confirmed
 91020 Stated holding company for all JW entertainment ventures. Accounts overdue Oct 2024. Used for post-PMCC SEIS solicitation. 104 shareholders, £200k capital.
12304231 BATH ATTRACTIONS LTD Active — Compliant 08 Nov 2019 Resigned Oct 2023 1 Argyle Street, Bath, BA2 4BA
↳ address changed from 8 The Circus
 91020 Holding company for House of Frankenstein visitor attraction. 138 shareholders at £1/share. JW resigned Oct 2023. Two active directors as of March 2026 — original director (from Nov 2019) and a second director appointed 15 May 2025. CH verified: 734,897 ordinary B shares allotted 6 Dec 2024, ratified by resolution 24 Mar 2025 — investors report not being notified of this allotment directly. Six historical confirmation statements re-filed Feb 2025 (2019–2024), suggesting corrective restatement of shareholding records. Posterity Entertainment Ltd ceased as PSC 13 Jan 2025. Accounts filed to Oct 2024 and Oct 2025 — fully compliant. A shareholder update in March 2026 reports visitor numbers up and new concessions planned, but no distribution of dividends or royalties, with the outlook described as cautious given the current economic climate.
12188280 PMCC LTD
prev: Full Metal Jurassic Ltd
 Liquidation 04 Sep 2019 Resigned Jan 2025 29 Craven Street, London, WC2N 5NT
↳ liquidator address; prev PSB Accountants NW9, prev 90 Victoria St Bristol, prev 8 The Circus Bath
 94990 Central TopCo. CVL commenced 28 Mar 2024; practitioner: TMP Restructuring. Statement of Affairs filed 7 May 2024 (12pp). Receipts & Payments filed 20 May 2025 (29pp) — both public on CH. Complaint CC-2023-115641. Director succession: Adam Leon (resigned Jul 2022) → interim co-director (resigned Jan 2023) → JW sole director. £116k DLA written off. Director's salary misrecorded as loan.
11995323 DUNE DRIFTER LTD Active — Strike-off Proposed 14 May 2019 Resigned Mar 2024 Lancaster Court, 36-39 Newman Street, London, W1T 1QH 59111 Mass resignation 4 Mar 2024. PSC transferred to the individual described as Edson Kajihara (alleged Brazilian national, Brazil resident) same day. Accounts overdue Feb 2025.
11881361 RUON RED LIMITED Dissolved 23 Jan 2024 14 Mar 2019 Dissolved Unit 7 Beech Avenue Business Park, Taverham, Norwich, NR8 6HW 62090 Dissolved 23 January 2024. SIC registered as IT services — used to sell China-based RUON crypto tokens. 53 shareholders. Last accounts to Mar 2021.
11120322 KA FILM LTD Active — Strike-off Proposed 20 Dec 2017 Resigned Oct 2023 Lancaster Court, 36-39 Newman Street, London, W1T 1QH 59111 Sole director: JW until 24 Oct 2023 (now no director). PMCC funds loaned here — undisclosed to investors. No charge registered. Accounts severely overdue (last filed Dec 2020). One investor's KA Film debt converted to Posterity shares. Film release and distribution: Killers Anonymous was distributed by Lionsgate and released June 2019. It grossed $100,581 internationally (Wikipedia / Box Office Mojo) and $0 in the US and Canada — however, it was available on DVD (confirmed: physical copies purchased at HMV) and on streaming platforms. As of March 2026 the film has 11,000+ IMDB ratings, indicating substantial viewership via DVD and streaming channels. Willis told investors that co-producer Grindstone Entertainment refused to share profits with KA Film, and that he had "Plan A, Plan B, and Plan C" to recover the money. He later told investors Plan A had not worked. No royalties were ever received by investors. Willis also told investors that Gary Oldman appeared for very little money — likely union minimum — as a moral debt, having been given his first major film break. At the investors, cast and crew screening, a rumour circulated that KA Films "had no money left." Whether Killers Anonymous generated profits that were withheld by Grindstone, or whether the film generated no distributable profit, has not been independently verified; investors believe this question warrants investigation by a journalist or regulator with access to Grindstone's accounts.
10685746 BLADUD SPRING LIMITED Active — Compliant 22 Mar 2017 Resigned Jan 2025 C/O Business Control Ltd, Red Lion Yard, Odd Down, Bath, BA2 2PP 11070 Product company selling Bath spring water ("Bath Water"). Described by investors as loss-making despite what should be a profitable premise. Fully compliant — accounts filed to Mar 2025. 25% JW shareholding.
10665548 FISTFUL OF LEAD LTD
prev: Maleficent Seven Ltd; Attack of the Sharks Ltd
 Active — Strike-off Proposed 13 Mar 2017 Resigned Mar 2024 Lancaster Court, 36-39 Newman Street, London, W1T 1QH 59111 Mass resignation 4 Mar 2024. PSC to the individual described as Edson Kajihara (alleged Brazilian national). Two prior names. Accounts overdue Dec 2024.
10554532 BATH HERITAGE LTD Active 10 Jan 2017 Resigned Feb 2017 11 Forester Road, Bathwick, Bath, BA2 6QF — JW director 10 Jan – 25 Feb 2017 only (6 weeks). No ongoing JW involvement.
10427464 ONE NIGHT IN BATH LTD Liquidation 13 Oct 2016 Resigned Mar 2024 Griffins Suite 011, Unit 2, 94a Wycliffe Road, Northampton, NN1 5JF
↳ liquidator address; prev Lancaster Court then Llandrinio Hall
 59111 Compulsory liquidation: petition 8 Jul 2024 (UKFF), winding-up order 27 Aug 2024, liquidator appointed 11 Oct 2024. Practitioner: Stephen Hunt (Griffins, IP 9183). Progress report Dec 2025 (document verified): £500k UKFF CLA (23 Mar 2021, matured 23 Mar 2024, no payments); £50k BBL Metro Bank Jun 2020 — liquidator states company does not appear entitled; £126,409.68 loan to Bladud Spring (connected by common directorship, Apr 2017) — Bladud balance sheet insolvent, enforcement ongoing; Willis bankrupt 15 Jan 2025 — liquidator has submitted claim against Willis for monies owed to ONIB; Kajihara interviewed, transfer investigation concluded (no financial benefit to creditors); Coutts and Metro Bank statements under investigation. Creditors: £1,176,879.50 (OR) / £1,232,113.04 claims received. Fee cap sought: £175,250. PSCs ceased 4 Mar 2024: JW, Christopher Harris, Posterity Entertainment Ltd. Kajihara appointed sole director same day, terminated 11 Mar 2025.
10020691 KUNG FU PRINCESS LTD Active — Strike-off Proposed 23 Feb 2016 Resigned Mar 2024 Lancaster Court, 36-39 Newman Street, London, W1T 1QH 59111 Film completed, released as video game "Mia and the Dragon Princess." 138 shareholders. Let's Be Evil shares rolled into KFP. PSC to the individual described as Edson Kajihara (alleged Brazilian national) 4 Mar 2024. Accounts overdue Nov 2023.
09905211 JOJOPA LIMITED Active — Compliant 07 Dec 2015 Resigned Jan 2025 The Methodist Church Tower Hill, Williton, Taunton, TA4 4NT
↳ address changed from Lancaster Court
 74909 Address updated from Newman St to Taunton Somerset. Accounts filed to Dec 2024 — fully compliant. 21 shareholders. £21k capital.
08988917 LET'S BE EVIL LTD Active — Strike-off Proposed 09 Apr 2014 Resigned Mar 2024 Lancaster Court, 36-39 Newman Street, London, W1T 1QH 59111 No shareholders listed at CH other than directors. Investor shares rolled into Kung Fu Princess. PSC to the individual described as Edson Kajihara (alleged Brazilian national) 4 Mar 2024. Accounts overdue Nov 2023.
07743579 DORIS SPINNING COMPANY (1920) LTD Dissolved — Dissolved (sole director) — — JW sole director, majority shareholder. 24 shareholders. Dissolved.
07426738 TORO PARK VILLAGE LIMITED Active — Compliant 02 Nov 2010 Shareholder only Suite 3, 12 Princess Street, Knutsford, Cheshire, WA16 6DD 55209 JW held 20% shareholding — not a director. Co-director Duncan Macdonald. £300k fixed assets. Accounts filed to Nov 2024 — compliant. Not directly investor-facing in this investigation.
07115959 THE IDEAS FACTORY (UK) LIMITED Dissolved — Dissolved (majority shareholder) — — JW majority shareholder. 19 shareholders. Had £800k shareholder funds. Published newsletter of business start-ups for subscribers to invest in. Possible predecessor to Special Situations Syndicate. Dissolved.
07065200 TALL PINES VILLAGE LIMITED Active — Compliant 03 Nov 2009 Resigned Jan 2025 Suite 3, 12 Princess Street, Knutsford, Cheshire, WA16 6DD 55209 JW director 5 Oct 2020 – 15 Jan 2025. Also director Nov 2009 – Jul 2014. Co-director Duncan Macdonald (active). £235k fixed assets. Not directly investor-facing in this investigation.
06924342 IOPTEC LIMITED Dissolved — Resigned Jun 2011 — — JW ~8% shareholder. Director resigned Jun 2011. 42 shareholders. Dissolved.
05912752 SPECIAL SITUATIONS SYNDICATE LIMITED Liquidation — Shareholder only (via MASI) — — JW held 200 shares via MASI. 51 shareholders. Conclusion of winding up 20 Mar 2024; due to be dissolved 2026. May be follow-on from The Ideas Factory newsletter vehicle.
05656128 VINE MEDIA LIMITED Dissolved — Resigned Jul 2014 — — JW director, 0 shares. Resigned Jul 2014. 1 shareholder. Dissolved.
04801710 REVERSIONARY PROPERTY CONSORTIUM LTD
prev: Reversionary Property Consortium PLC (2004–2021); No.1 Ltd (2003)
 Active — Compliant 17 Jun 2003 Resigned Mar 2007 176-178 Pontefract Road, Cudworth, Barnsley, S72 8BE 68100 JW resigned Mar 2007. Two previous names including a PLC period. Accounts filed to Mar 2025 — compliant. Property investment vehicle from early career.
04621111 BOOKSENDER LIMITED Dissolved 22 Nov 2011 18 Dec 2002 Resigned Dec 2009 Bron Y Bryn, Kerry, Newtown, Powys, SY16 4NY 5247 Dissolved 22 November 2011. JW resigned Dec 2009. Shares transferred via JW and MASI to third party. Powys address — consistent with Wales connection.

Manages 25 Lancaster Court, Newman St — the shared registered office address. London flats subsequently repossessed. Accounts filed to Mar 2025 — compliant.
03854765 I M N LIMITED
prev: Oakdale Technical Services Ltd (1999)
