03403434 M.A.S.I. LTD
prev: Marches Architectural Salvage Ltd (1997–2004)
 Active — Strike-off Proposed 15 Jul 1997 Resigned Mar 2024 · remains PSC Lynwood House, 373-375 Station Road, Harrow, HA1 2AW 46130, 68320, 82990 Originally architectural salvage company — repurposed as investment vehicle. Used for Manhattan loan scheme (2018, £4k). Bank account used for RUON tokens. Director resigned 4 Mar 2024 but remains PSC. Accounts overdue Jul 2024.
02721400 BLACKS LIMITED
prev: Covellan Limited (1992)
 Liquidation 09 Jun 1992 Resigned Jan 2025 29 Craven Street, London, WC2N 5NT
↳ liquidator address; prev 67 Dean St, prev Jubilee House NW9
 56101, 94990 Subsidiary members club. Closed Oct 2023. £10k revenue vs £20k weekly cost. JW and a co-director appointed 4 May 2022. Co-director resigned 24 Jan 2023; JW sole director until 15 Jan 2025. CVL: extraordinary resolution 4 Dec 2023; liquidator appointed 11 Dec 2023. PMCC Ltd was notified as PSC 4 May 2022 — confirming formal corporate control link. Two LIQ03 receipts & payments filed (Dec 2024, Dec 2025) — both public on CH. Four charges satisfied Aug 2022.
— PEUT TOKENS
Crypto token — not a registered UK company
 Token · Not Registered — — — — PEUT tokens were offered to investors as a crypto investment vehicle. Listed on exchanges. Apparent current value: £0.00 with no visible trading history. Not associated with any registered UK company. Investors who purchased PEUT tokens have received no return and have no recourse through standard CH mechanisms.

31 entities · All statuses verified live from Companies House March 2026 · CH officer IDs: NR602guK9ZuIldVC6AmxBoPjQ7U & _SwZ8LI9dEFwanu0ULGbGKVK-RI · Pooled investor testimony and correspondence

Section 07

Documented
Bank Accounts

Five bank accounts have been documented by the activist group as recipients of investor funds across multiple investment vehicles. This data was compiled for submission to liquidators TMP to enable transaction history investigation.

Investment · Sep 2020

MASI LTD

Sort: 08-92-99 · Acc: ████████

Used Sept 2020 for "RUON Tokens & Equity in Ruon Marketing Ltd." Also used for the Manhattan penthouse loan scheme c.2018. Investor paid £4,000 into this account; funds never returned. Shares later converted to equity in other JW ventures.

Investment · Oct 2021

POSTERITY LTD

Sort: 23-05-80 · Acc: ████████

Used Oct 2021 for "PlanetX Metaverse Tokens." Same sort code as all other Posterity-linked accounts — suggesting a common banking relationship across multiple vehicles.

Investment · Oct 2021

POSTERITY CLUB

Sort: 23-05-80 · Acc: ████████

Used Oct 2021 for "Posterity Digital Investment Club." Club was never launched. Funds used elsewhere. Written confirmation of this exists from both Adam Leon and the director's wife. Basis of direct fraud claim by at least one investor. Notably: in January 2023 — ten months before the collapse — an investor was told in writing by Adam Leon that their £3,000 PDIC investment was "now potentially worth around £4,500," sitting in "stable and less risky assets." The fund had never been established. Investors contend this valuation — provided at the moment questions were being asked — served to deter withdrawal.

Investment · Oct 2021

PMCC LTD (NFT)

Sort: 23-05-80 · Acc: ████████

Used Oct 2021 for "Crypto Club Membership NFT." Same account also used Jan 2022 for SEIS equity. Investors have formally requested disclosure of all ETH/BTC wallet addresses as crypto payments may not have been converted to fiat and deposited here.

Investment · Jan 2022

PMCC LTD (SEIS)

Sort: 23-05-80 · Acc: ████████

Used Jan 2022 for "Crypto-Club Global SEIS Equity." Same account as NFT round. SEIS relief used to attract investment. Despite PMCC positioning as a crypto club, the liquidator declared zero crypto assets — inconsistency formally raised with TMP.

Section 08

Alleged
Aliases

Three confirmed aliases have been independently corroborated by sources with direct knowledge. A fourth name — appearing on public Companies House filings — is also documented here, with questions raised about whether it represents a genuine individual or a further assumed identity.

"Maria"

First documented appearance in this investigation: November 2023 — within weeks of Blacks collapsing and concurrent with the filing of the Insolvency Service complaint. However, multiple investors report that the name "Maria" had been used in correspondence for some years prior to this, predating the collapse. The earliest documented use on record is the November 2023 refund deflection, but this may not represent the first deployment of the persona. An investor pursuing return of PDIC funds received a reply signed "Maria" stating the subject was "currently ill (heart condition)" and "convalescing," with no further substantive response ever provided. The persona continued to be deployed for investor communications after co-director Adam Leon departed. A source with direct personal knowledge of the subject confirmed he told them he used a female name because it was "easier to buy from." Multiple investors attempted contact by phone and video without success. "Maria" was actively approaching investors in June 2025, promoting House of Frankenstein shares. Confirmed by separate source as almost certainly the director himself.

"Peter Parfait"

Used c.2008–2011 as editor of property investment newsletters "Property Auction News" and "Hot Property News" — own website peterparfait.com (archived via Wayback Machine). A long-term acquaintance with 15+ years of dealings independently confirmed this alias without prompting. Archived site design language matches Posterity Entertainment publications identically. Adam Leon also linked to the same Llandrinio Hall address (RUON Marketing Ltd, CH: 13143833 — dissolved 2022).

Fictitious Name (Wells)

A source with direct knowledge of the subject's movements reported in December 2025 that he is allegedly living in Wells, Somerset, under a fictitious name following separation from his wife. The subject emigrated to Dubai (confirmed June 2025) and may be moving between locations. This is the most recently reported UK presence — reported by a single source and not independently verified.

UNVERIFIED IDENTITY

"Edson Kajihara"

A name appearing on public UK Companies House filings. On 4 March 2024 — the same day as the mass resignations — this individual was not merely listed as PSC but was formally appointed as the sole director of One Night in Bath Ltd, where he remained on the register as director until 11 March 2025. He also received PSC in Dune Drifter Ltd, Fistful of Lead Ltd, and One Night in Bath Ltd — all via the Lancaster Court, 36–39 Newman Street, W1T 1QH registered office used across the Willis network for years. The ONIB liquidator's progress report (December 2025, document verified) confirms that Kajihara was interviewed as part of the investigation. The liquidator formally investigated the transfer of the company to Kajihara and has concluded that investigation, stating there is no prospect it will bring financial benefit to creditors. This establishes that Kajihara is a real, identifiable person who was accessible to and interviewed by the liquidator, and that the transfer has been examined and closed as a line of inquiry. This investigation does not assert that "Edson Kajihara" is a pseudonym or alias of Jonathan Willis. The facts documented here — for the attention of the Insolvency Service and Companies House — are: (1) no independent public verification of his identity or background has been possible by this investigation; (2) the address used was the subject's own longstanding registered office; (3) his appointment was simultaneous with a coordinated mass exit; (4) no contact with this individual has been established by any investor or correspondent outside the liquidation process; (5) he held the role of sole director, not merely PSC — a position carrying legal responsibilities he will have been required to account for to the liquidator.

Section 09

Chronology
of Events

c. 2000 — earliest entity

I M N Limited — First Recorded Corporate Connection

I M N Limited (03854765, prev: Oakdale Technical Services Ltd) is incorporated October 1999. The subject is listed as a shareholder. The company later enters liquidation, ordered by the court. Annual returns have been overdue since 2001. This represents the earliest identified corporate connection — the beginning of a 25-year corporate trail.

Companies House · 03854765

2003–2011 · Early investment vehicles

Property, Books, and Early Investment Newsletters

The subject incorporates Booksender Ltd (retail books, Powys address, dissolved 2011), Reversionary Property Consortium Ltd (now active, Barnsley), and Vine Media Ltd (dissolved). The "Peter Parfait" newsletter era — Property Auction News and The Ideas Factory — runs from this period. The Ideas Factory (07115959) publishes a newsletter of business start-ups for subscribers to invest in; it has £800k in shareholder funds before dissolving. The Manhattan penthouse loan pitch through MASI Ltd (originally Marches Architectural Salvage Ltd, incorporated 1997) is traced to around 2018, with investors still chasing those funds in 2025.

Companies House · Investor testimony · Wayback Machine archive Free shares pattern begins

2014–2019 · Film network established

Film Companies Incorporated — Investors Recruited Into Multiple Vehicles

Let's Be Evil Ltd (2014), One Night in Bath Ltd (2016), Kung Fu Princess Ltd (2016), Fistful of Lead Ltd (2017, prev: Attack of the Sharks / Maleficent Seven), Bath Heritage Ltd (2017, resigned 6 weeks later), Bladud Spring Ltd (2017), and Dune Drifter Ltd (2019) are incorporated in rapid succession. All are registered at Lancaster Court, Newman Street — a shared registered office. Several films were released on DVD and/or streaming platforms but none received a UK cinema release. Investors describe being kept waiting years with promises of imminent cinema release. Lost investors in one film are offered shares in the next. One Night in Bath never reached DVD. Kung Fu Princess was not released as a film; it was repurposed as an interactive game under the title Mia and the Dragon Princess.

Companies House · Investor spreadsheet Free shares cycle: Let's Be Evil → Kung Fu Princess

2019–2021 · Crypto and token era

RUON Tokens, PlanetX NFTs, and Crypto Club Global

RUON Red Ltd (IT services SIC, China-based tokens, 53 shareholders) and RUON Marketing Ltd (Llandrinio Hall address, dissolved Dec 2022) are used to sell tokens and equity. PlanetX Metaverse tokens are offered via Posterity Ltd bank account Oct 2021. Crypto Club Global NFT memberships are sold via PMCC Ltd bank account Oct 2021. Posterity Digital Investment Club funds collected Oct 2021 — club never launched. Written confirmation of this provided to investors by the director's wife and co-director Adam Leon.

NFT Life Membership Revenue — Unaccounted Funds

In December 2021, Crypto Club Global launched a Genesis Collection of 923 NFTs on OpenSea (Ethereum blockchain), marketed as life membership passes granting access to physical and virtual private members' clubhouses, including a flagship London location operating under the Blacks members' club brand.

At an average mint price of 0.15 ETH per NFT, the initial minting generated an estimated 138.45 ETH in primary sales — approximately $553,800 USD at December 2021 Ethereum prices (~$4,000/ETH). Secondary market trading on OpenSea has since recorded over 114 ETH in total volume, generating further royalty income flowing back to the collection's creator wallet.

While members did initially receive access to the benefits their NFT membership entitled them to, this proved shortlived — Blacks closed within months of many purchasing their life memberships, rendering those passes effectively worthless.

What remains unclear is the full disposition of funds raised. The total proceeds from both primary minting and ongoing secondary market royalties are uncertain across identified financial records, and it has not been established where all funds generated through this collection were directed or how they were applied within the business prior to closure.

These figures are estimates based on publicly available OpenSea and Ethereum blockchain data. An on-chain forensic trace of the minting wallet would be required to confirm exact receipts and fund movements.

Bank accounts documented · Investor testimony Free shares cycle: RUON tokens → Posterity shares

Late 2022

PMCC Investment Round — Investors Misled About Financial Position

The subject and Adam Leon raise funds from investors for PMCC Ltd (TopCo for Blacks members' club). Multiple investors are explicitly told the business is breaking even. Specific assurances are given that investment will not be used to cover existing liabilities. Late-stage investors are offered double shares — described as screwing over earlier investors. Memberships are reportedly sold the same day the club closes.

Email correspondence · Investor testimony

19 July 2022 · 24 January 2023

Director Succession — Leon → Interim Co-Director → Willis Sole Director

Companies House confirms Adam Leon's formal directorship of PMCC Ltd ended on 19 July 2022, when he was replaced as director by a co-director who in turn resigned on 24 January 2023, leaving Willis as sole director. Despite his formal resignation in July 2022, Adam Leon continued acting for the company through mid-2023 — contacting investors, providing investment updates, and identifying himself as "Communications Director — Posterity" — well after he had formally ceased to be a CH-registered director. On 24 October 2023 — the same day he is copied on a formal investor refund demand — Leon sends an email formally distancing himself, stating: "I have acted as an administrator and communications manager... I have had nothing to do with the management of this scheme whatsoever. I have had no control over funds or their investment or access to bank accounts." This statement directly contradicts two years of signed correspondence in which he provided investment performance updates investors have characterised as false. As recently as January 2023, Leon had written to an investor confirming their PDIC funds were "now potentially worth around £4,500" — for a fund that had never been established. He directs all queries to Willis as "Director."

Companies House · PMCC filing history · authenticated investor correspondence

May–July 2023

Dubai Expansion, Freehold Offer — Fundraising Continues Into Terminal Decline

A "Blacks Dubai Deck" is circulated. Investors are offered the Blacks freehold for purchase. Dubai club memberships are sold (July 2023 newsletter). The subject is photographed on a yacht in Dubai "exploring the Dubai club offerings." Fundraising continues while the club is, it will emerge, losing £10,000 per week.

Email correspondence · Social media photographs

16 October 2023

Blacks Collapses — Subject Sends Mass Apology Revealing True Financial Picture

Blacks Ltd ceases trading. The subject circulates a letter revealing for the first time: £10,000 weekly revenue vs £20,000 weekly cost; £270,000+ inherited debt; sixteen months of continuous subsidy. He states "through dozens of businesses over the past 61 years, none other of which has ever gone under." Investors note this directly contradicts assurances given at investment. Within days, he resigns from Bath Attractions Ltd and ONIB SPV Ltd.

Subject email to shareholders · 16 Oct 2023

4 December 2023

Blacks Ltd — Creditors Voluntary Liquidation · PMCC Confirmed as Former PSC

Companies House confirms Blacks Ltd passed an extraordinary resolution to wind up on 4 December 2023, with a voluntary liquidator appointed on 11 December 2023. A Statement of Affairs was filed the same day. Two subsequent Liquidators' Statements of Receipts and Payments have been filed — to December 2024 (32 pages) and December 2025 (30 pages) — both publicly available on CH. Notably, CH also confirms that PMCC Ltd was notified as a Person with Significant Control of Blacks Ltd on 4 May 2022 — the same day Willis and a co-director were appointed as directors. This establishes a formal corporate control link between the two companies that was not previously documented in public sources; investors were investing in PMCC, which itself controlled the underlying Blacks operating entity. CH also confirms four charges against Blacks Ltd were satisfied in August 2022, shortly after the Willis/PMCC acquisition.

Companies House · Blacks filing history · 02721400 · CH verified March 2026

November 2023

"Maria" Deployed for First Time — Heart Condition Claimed to Deflect Refund Demand

Within weeks of the Blacks collapse, an investor pursuing return of Posterity Digital Investment Club funds received correspondence signed "Maria," stating the subject was "currently ill (heart condition)" and "in convalescence." No substantive response followed. This is the earliest documented appearance of the persona in this investigation — though multiple investors report the name had been used in correspondence for some years prior to 2023, suggesting this was not the first deployment. The persona was deployed here to deflect a refund demand at the moment of maximum legal exposure, concurrent with the filing of the Insolvency Service complaint. The same medical-incapacity narrative would be repeated in March 2024, when the subject claimed via WhatsApp to be hospitalised while five Companies House filings were processed under his officer ID in the same window. By June 2025 he was confirmed to have emigrated to Dubai.

Investor correspondence · November 2023 · authenticated

25 November 2023

Insolvency Service Complaint Filed — CC-2023-115641

Formal complaint submitted against PMCC Ltd (12188280). Five specific allegations: misrepresentation at point of investment; misuse of funds; failure to communicate with shareholders; irregular shareholder certificate signed by a resigned director; failure to inform shareholders of insolvency status. Investigation confirmed ongoing as of March 2025.

Insolvency Service · CC-2023-115641

28 March 2024

PMCC Ltd — Creditors Voluntary Liquidation Commences · Statement of Affairs Filed

Companies House confirms PMCC Ltd entered Creditors Voluntary Liquidation (CVL) on 28 March 2024, following an extraordinary resolution to wind up passed the same day. Practitioner: TMP Restructuring, appointed 28 March 2024. A Statement of Affairs (12 pages) was filed 7 May 2024. A Liquidators' Statement of Receipts and Payments (29 pages) was filed 20 May 2025 — both are publicly available on Companies House. The CVL filing followed a First Gazette notice for compulsory strike-off issued 12 March 2024, indicating HMRC or another creditor had already taken action before the voluntary liquidation was initiated.

Companies House · PMCC filing history · 12188280 · CH verified March 2026

4 March 2024

Mass Resignation — Five Companies, One Day. Edson Kajihara Takes Control.

Five simultaneous director resignations filed. Edson Kajihara appointed as director (not merely PSC) of One Night in Bath, and as PSC of three entities — all via the shared Lancaster Court address. CH confirms three PSCs ceased simultaneously at ONIB: Jonathan Willis, Christopher Harris, and Posterity Entertainment Ltd itself. Notably, the subject had sent an investor a WhatsApp message from hospital on 1 March and claimed to still be "really bad" on 5 March — yet five CH filings were processed in this window.

Companies House mass filings · 4 Mar 2024 · CH verified March 2026

6 December 2024

Bath Attractions Ltd — 734,897 New B Shares Issued — Investors Not Notified

Companies House confirms that Bath Attractions Ltd (12304231) issued 734,897 ordinary B shares on 6 December 2024. The allotment was ratified by shareholder resolution on 24 March 2025, with pre-emption rights waived. An investor in the company reports learning of this allotment only through Companies House — no direct communication was received. The CH filing history also shows that six historical confirmation statements (covering 2019–2024) were re-filed in February 2025, suggesting corrective restatements of the company's shareholding records across multiple years. Posterity Entertainment Ltd separately ceased as a Person with Significant Control of Bath Attractions on 13 January 2025, filed 29 May 2025. A second director was appointed 15 May 2025. The failure to notify existing shareholders of a significant new share allotment — particularly one that dilutes existing holdings — is a legitimate governance concern.

Companies House · Bath Attractions Ltd · 12304231 · CH verified March 2026 · investor testimony

June 2025

Subject Confirmed Emigrated to Dubai — Properties Lost — New Fundraising Reported

A source with direct knowledge confirms the subject has emigrated to Dubai. Evicted from The Circus, Bath. London flats at Lancaster Court repossessed. Hertfordshire property failed to sell. Wife and daughter staying with friends. IVA reportedly entered. Simultaneously, "Maria" persona approaches investors with House of Frankenstein shares (100,000 shares at 20p) — consistent with step one of a new recruitment cycle.

Source testimony · Email activist group Jun 2025 Free shares cycle: new vehicle offering

17 December 2025

ONIB Liquidator Files 35-Page Progress Report — Two Government Loans, Bladud Debt, Willis Creditor Claim

The ONIB liquidator (Stephen Hunt, Griffins) files a 35-page progress report (WU07), covering 11 October 2024 to 10 October 2025. Key document-confirmed findings: (1) ONIB received a £500,000 UKFF convertible loan (CLA dated 23 March 2021, matured 23 March 2024, no payments made — basis of winding-up petition); (2) ONIB received a £50,000 Bounce Back Loan from Metro Bank in June 2020 — liquidator states the company does not seem to have been entitled to apply for and receive the loan; (3) ONIB made a loan of £126,409.68 to Bladud Spring (connected by common directorship, dated 5 April 2017) — Bladud is balance sheet insolvent, enforcement ongoing; (4) Willis was made bankrupt on 15 January 2025 — the liquidator has submitted a claim against Willis in his bankruptcy estate for monies Willis owes the Company (not the reverse); (5) Edson Kajihara was interviewed by the liquidator; the transfer investigation has been concluded with no financial benefit to creditors; (6) bank accounts were at Coutts and Metro Bank — transactions under investigation; (7) Prism 339 Ltd engaged for bank statement conversion at £600 fixed fee. Creditors total: £1,176,879.50 per OR; claims received £1,232,113.04. No dividend can currently be estimated.

Companies House · WU07 · ONIB 10427464 · filed 17 Dec 2025 · CH verified March 2026

9 January 2026

ONIB Creditors' Meeting (Microsoft Teams) — Investor Group Not Fully Notified

A creditors' meeting for One Night in Bath Ltd is held via Microsoft Teams. Some investors within the activist group were unaware of this meeting, raising concerns about whether all creditors were adequately notified in accordance with the liquidation process. This contrasts with the PMCC liquidation, where creditor communications were more widely distributed. Any ONIB investors who were not notified may wish to contact the liquidator's office (Griffins, Northampton) to ensure they are on the creditor list and receive future correspondence.

Investor testimony · January 2026

December 2025

"Peter Parfait" Confirmed — "BINGO!! JACKPOT" — Fundraising Continuing

Wayback Machine evidence identifies identical design language between peterparfait.com and Posterity publications. Long-term acquaintance independently confirms "Peter Parfait" as a known alias. Separate source reports the subject is now in Wells, Somerset, under a fictitious name and "trying to raise money again." Group plans escalation to FCA, NCA, and Action Fraud in 2026.

Wayback Machine · Source testimony · Dec 2025

15 January 2025

Final Resignations — All Active Directorships Vacated

Subject resigns from all remaining active CH roles: PMCC Ltd (liquidation), Blacks Ltd (liquidation), Posterity Entertainment Ltd (strike-off proposed), Bladud Spring Ltd, Tall Pines Village Ltd, and JoJopa Ltd. Both officer IDs on Companies House now show all roles as resigned. The subject holds no active UK directorships on record as of March 2026. The ONIB liquidator's progress report (December 2025) confirms Willis was made bankrupt on 15 January 2025 — consistent with the resignation date and with investor reports of property losses and an IVA.

Companies House · both officer IDs · 15 Jan 2025

March 2026 · Confirmed Active

Three Investment Pitches in Two Weeks — "Peter Parfait" Mailing List Operational

While PMCC Ltd remains in liquidation and investors await any return of funds, p@peterparfait.com circulates four distinct investment solicitations: (1) an RWA property tokenisation platform inviting property owners and investor "partners"; (2) a semi-off-grid survivalist countryside estate in the South West, limited to 20 participants; (3) the Global Wellness Sanctuary Initiative — a network of luxury wellness retreats across seven countries, powered by a proprietary "wellness utility token"; and (4) a film financing opportunity explicitly described as "zero risk" with "guaranteed global distribution." All four are signed "Peter Parfait," route to the same email address, name no registered company, carry no FCA authorisation, and contain no prospectus or risk warning. The fourth pitch additionally uses language — "zero risk" and "guaranteed" — that, if the communication constitutes a regulated financial promotion, would engage COBS 4.2 as a named category of non-compliant content. Whether it qualifies is a matter for the FCA to assess. These pitches are documented in full in Section 13.

Emails received March 2026 · p@peterparfait.com · authenticated Cycle 5: active solicitation

Section 10

Shareholder
Stories

All accounts drawn from testimony and correspondence shared by more than a dozen investors across the activist group, 2023–2025. All identifying names removed. Amounts and details as reported.

Story 01 · Lead Complainant

"I asked directly — was told it was breaking even"

Invested a substantial five-figure sum alongside a family member after being explicitly assured the business was breaking even. Specifically asked whether investment would cover existing liabilities; assured it would not. Discovered the true financial position only after collapse. Filed Insolvency Service complaint CC-2023-115641. Primary coordinator of the activist group. Primary liaison with liquidators TMP. Has submitted detailed evidence including the full corporate network analysis.

£23,000+ · PMCC shares

Story 02 · Family Group Investor

"My loan was used to fund a £8,479 director payment — then rolled into a meme coin offer"

Family group collectively invested £42,500 in PMCC. Separately loaned funds to MASI Ltd in March 2022 after being told it was a "100% safe and secure bridging loan for 2–3 months." Funds never returned; later told they had been used in other businesses "as things were tight." Notes that £8,479 was repaid to the sole director from MASI accounts in August 2022, potentially funded by this loan. Offered debts swapped for Spain development, NFT memberships, and Mickey meme coin. Identifies Mickey meme coin as a potential pump-and-dump: the director initially said a market maker would withdraw funds immediately on listing, then later said he couldn't liquidate until $10m market cap — a direct contradiction.

£42,500 family · PMCC + MASI

Story 03 · Long-Term Investor (dealings since 2006)

"A spider's web — one company's finances wrapped in another in an impenetrable way"

Longest documented relationship with the subject, dating to 2006. Invested in MASI (2018 Manhattan loan, £4,000), PMCC shares, One Night in Bath Ltd (since c.2017), Let's Be Evil (shares rolled into KFP), Kung Fu Princess, and KA Film (converted to Posterity shares). Identifies the pattern explicitly: new venture launched just before prior one is exposed, reducing investor scepticism. Describes Posterity as designed to "sweep up lossmakers" — but no lossmaker has been swept up. Was an extra in the last scene of One Night in Bath (summer 2023). Independently confirmed "Peter Parfait" as a known alias dating to the property newsletter era. Also introduced the "Wealth Factory" newsletter as a related vehicle.

Significant losses across 5+ entities

Story 04 · Crypto Industry Professional

"Memberships sold the same day they closed the doors — double shares for late investors"

Works in crypto; invested c.£2,000–£2,500 in a founders membership and subsequently £6,000 in PMCC shares. Days after investing, requested return of funds when a personal circumstance arose — was told funds had "already been spent." Co-director was condescending: "staff need to get paid." Notes heavy censorship of Blacks WhatsApp channels — outside content deleted, members blocked. Late-stage investors offered double shares, "utterly outrageous and screwing over earlier supporters." Describes management culture as "pompous and toxic." Reports hearing that Hogarths and artworks from the club's collection "disappeared."

£6,000 PMCC + £2,500 membership

Story 05 · Financial Professional

"Ultra vires — the salary misrepresentation would not survive IR35 scrutiny"

Has financial background. Identifies the KA Film loan as potentially ultra vires — beyond the director's legal authority. States directly that the director's characterisation of salary as "self-employed fees" would not survive IR35/PAYE scrutiny. Is considering court action for misappropriation of Dean Street tokenisation funds. Notes the crypto/metaverse concept was "kicked into touch without a word of explanation" to investors who had invested in that premise specifically. Invested via Posterity email list having dealt with the directors previously — acknowledges "most of their projects have unwound for the worse since then."

Amount not disclosed

Story 06 · PDIC Investor / Fraud Claim

"The club was never launched — confirmed in writing. The funds were misappropriated."

Invested across RUON tokens (2019), RUON Marketing equity (Oct 2020), PlanetX NFTs (Sep 2021), PMCC NFT membership (Oct 2021), PMCC SEIS equity (Jan 2022), and Posterity Digital Investment Club (Oct 2021). PDIC funds collected via dedicated bank account — club never launched. Has written confirmation from both Adam Leon and the director's wife. Is pursuing a direct fraud claim. Separately maintains documentation of "Maria" email correspondence, shared with the group. Authenticated email records show that in January 2023 — with the fund non-existent — Adam Leon wrote to confirm the investment was "now potentially worth around £4,500," funds described as sitting in "stable and less risky assets." This valuation was provided at a time the fund had never been established; the investor contends it deterred withdrawal at a critical moment. When refund was formally requested in late 2023, correspondence was answered by "Maria" — who cited the subject's heart condition as reason for delay — with no substantive response ever following.

£20,000+ across multiple vehicles

Story 07 · Direct-Knowledge Source

"He told me personally — a female name is easier to buy from"

Has direct personal knowledge of the subject's business methods. Confirmed "Maria" alias to the group, with the subject having made the comment directly to this person. Also confirmed Dubai emigration, Bath eviction, London flat repossessions, and the IVA. In December 2025 reported the subject is in Wells, Somerset, under a fictitious name, separated from wife, and "trying to raise money again." Confirmed Lancaster Court property connection. Declined to be named publicly.

Amount not disclosed

Story 08 · Peter Parfait Investigator

"Same font, same layout — I recognised it immediately from the Posterity materials"

Signed up to "Property Auction News" newsletter in the late 2000s. Used the Wayback Machine to recover peterparfait.com, identifying identical design language to Posterity Entertainment materials — same font, same layout, same visual style. Shared screenshots with the activist group. Subsequently corroborated by a long-term acquaintance independently. Lost over £20,000 across JW vehicles. Was separately in correspondence with the subject's estranged wife regarding his whereabouts and current activities.

£20,000+ · Multiple JW vehicles

Story 09 · Crypto Wallet Evidence Request

"Provide all Ethereum and Bitcoin wallet addresses — and the full blockchain transaction history"

Formally requested, in addition to bank statements, a full list of all cryptocurrency wallet addresses used by PMCC and the complete blockchain transaction history up to point of fiat conversion. Request submitted to TMP liquidators. The concern: crypto wallet anonymity enables concealment of assets that bank records alone cannot reveal. PMCC operated as a crypto club yet declared zero crypto assets to the liquidator — a discrepancy investors believe warrants a specific technical investigation.

Formal evidence request submitted to TMP

Story 10 · Early NFT Member

"I was told PMCC didn't carry any of the costs for running Blacks — yet it clearly did"

Purchased founder NFT membership in 2021. Subsequently invested in PMCC shares July 2022 after receiving prospectus describing PMCC as the holding company for global club expansion, with funds exclusively for growth — not day-to-day operations. Co-director's May 2023 email explicitly stated: "PMCC doesn't carry any of the costs for running Blacks." The subject's October 2023 letter then confirms PMCC subsidised Blacks' operations for sixteen months — a direct contradiction. Also invested in the Posterity Digital Investment Club and focused recourse efforts on those funds.

PMCC shares + PDIC investment

Section 11 · Live Evidence · June 2025

"Maria" & the
House of Frankenstein

In June 2025 — while PMCC was in formal liquidation and the Insolvency Service investigation was ongoing — an investor received an unsolicited approach from "Maria" about a distressed sale opportunity in House of Frankenstein (the Bath visitor attraction held via Bath Attractions Ltd). An investor with 100,000 shares was said to be looking to exit at 20p per share. The investor shared this exchange with the activist group and immediately identified the hallmarks of the same recurring pattern: a venture described as doing extremely well, yet with a motivated seller urgently seeking to exit before, in their words, "the ship sinks."

⚑ Sales Technique Analysis — "Maria" / House of Frankenstein Approach

Technique 01 · False Scarcity

The "distressed sale" framing creates artificial urgency — a motivated seller needing to exit quickly. This pressures the target into a swift decision rather than allowing time for due diligence. The same technique was used in 2022 when late PMCC investors were told only a few shares remained.

Technique 02 · Success Narrative Contradicted by Exit Behaviour

The venture is described as "doing extremely well" — yet an insider is urgently selling 100,000 shares at 20p. If the business were genuinely thriving, an insider would hold, not exit at speed. The contradiction between the narrative and the seller's behaviour is the tell. Investors noted exactly this pattern at Blacks: told it was breaking even while costs doubled revenue.

Technique 03 · Intermediary as Buffer

"Maria" operates as a layer of separation from direct accountability. The investor notes they have never been able to speak to Maria by phone or video. No verifiable identity has ever been established. When the investor pushed for information about their other outstanding investment, "Maria" stopped responding entirely — consistent with a persona designed to deflect rather than resolve.

Technique 04 · New Opportunity to Absorb Old Debt

The approach came to an investor already owed money from prior JW investments. Offering them a new discounted opportunity serves two purposes: it generates fresh cash and keeps the creditor engaged in a new vehicle rather than pressing for repayment of the old debt. The investor recognised this and attempted to use it as leverage to recover prior funds — at which point "Maria" went silent.

Technique 05 · Female Persona to Lower Guard

A source with direct personal knowledge confirmed that the subject told them he used a female name because it was "easier to buy from." The investor group independently concluded that "Maria" is almost certainly the subject himself — no meeting, phone call or video has ever been produced to confirm otherwise. The persona has been used consistently for all investor communications since co-director Adam Leon's departure in January 2023.

Technique 06 · IVA as Leverage / Debt Management

An investor confirmed receiving a voice note from the subject in which he asked to have their debt kept outside the IVA, with personal assurances of repayment. This is significant: it suggests the subject was aware of which creditors were most likely to pursue him, and was managing them individually with verbal assurances that carry no legal weight — while simultaneously initiating an IVA that would extinguish other debts.

Technique 07 · Medical Sympathy as Evasion

"Maria" introduced herself to at least one investor by explaining the subject was "currently ill (heart condition)" and convalescing — making it functionally impossible to press a refund demand without appearing callous. The same tactic was deployed independently in March 2024, when the subject claimed via WhatsApp to be hospitalised ("really bad") during the exact window in which five Companies House filings were processed under his officer ID. Within months of the November 2023 "heart condition" claim, the subject was confirmed to have emigrated to Dubai. The medical frailty narrative appears to function as a pause mechanism — buying time, deflecting creditors, and generating sympathy — rather than reflecting genuine incapacity. Its use on two separate occasions, months apart, suggests a deliberate pattern rather than coincidence.

"Another business that is supposedly doing extremely well, yet there is an investor with 100,000 shares looking to exit for 20p a share. I suspect that this is classic Jonathan trying to exit and make some money before the ship sinks."

— Investor, activist group correspondence, June 2025

"I do wonder who Maria is though as she seems to be the person sending emails ever since Adam left the scene. I've never managed to speak to her either and so I wonder if it is just an alias."

— Investor, activist group correspondence, June 2025

"He wanted to keep my debt out of the IVA and assured me that he would repay it (not that I have any reason to believe he would). Most of our messages are via WhatsApp and it is just years of empty promises and kicking the can down the road."

— Investor, activist group correspondence, June 2025. Voice note in possession of investor group.

"He had offered to swap or exchange the debt for other projects such as the project in Spain or the Mickey meme coin too."

— Investor, activist group correspondence, June 2025

⊕ Additional Evidence · Prospective Investor Exchange · Forwarded 15 March 2026

The Adam Leon Angle — "We Are Merely the Messenger"

A separate email exchange, forwarded to the investigation on 15 March 2026, reveals that the House of Frankenstein distressed sale was not only being promoted by "Maria" — it was simultaneously being brokered by Adam Leon directly, using his personal email address ([email redacted]). The target in this exchange, identified here as "the prospective investor," was an investor who conducted proper due diligence and uncovered details that Adam Leon had not volunteered. The exchange is one of the most significant pieces of evidence in the investigation: it shows both the mechanics of the share sale and the context of the bankruptcy — which was not disclosed by Leon unprompted.

⚑ Key Revelation 01 · Adam Leon Still Active

Adam Leon — who resigned as PMCC co-director in January 2023 and later claimed to have been "merely an administrator" — was actively brokering the sale of House of Frankenstein shares in June 2025, more than two years after his stated departure. He states: "we are brokering this sale… it is purely a favour." The use of "we" indicates he is acting on behalf of Willis or the network, not independently.

⚑ Key Revelation 02 · Founder Shares Transferred Pre-Bankruptcy

750,000 of the founders' 1,500,000 'A shares' in Bath Attractions Ltd (HOF) were transferred to a named creditor — described as the lead construction contractor who had not been fully paid. Adam Leon confirms this transfer occurred in January 2025, coinciding with what the prospective investor's due diligence team identified as a personal bankruptcy filing. The prospective investor states in writing: "I understand one of the founders was declared bankrupt in January 2025, which obviously coincides with the transfer of shares."

⚑ Key Revelation 03 · Shares as Payment for Unpaid Construction Work

Leon confirms the lead contractor for HOF construction was not fully paid in cash and accepted shares from Posterity Entertainment as consideration — the same pattern documented across the entire network: debts not repaid in cash, converted instead into equity stakes. That creditor is now reportedly trying to exit those shares at an 80% discount, suggesting little confidence in their value.

⚑ Key Revelation 04 · Potential Fraudulent Preference / Antecedent Transaction

The transfer of 750,000 founder shares to a creditor immediately before or coinciding with a personal bankruptcy filing raises serious legal questions. The prospective investor's due diligence team flagged this directly: "there could be legal implications and consequences for both buyer and seller (as well as anyone aiding such a transaction who is aware of this situation)." Under UK insolvency law, asset transfers made prior to bankruptcy to preferred creditors can be challenged as fraudulent preferences or transactions at an undervalue.

⚑ Key Revelation 05 · "Profitable But Not Declaring Dividends"

Leon's pitch states HOF is "currently trading profitably but is not declaring dividends until accrued losses are cleared from set up." This is the same framing used repeatedly across JW ventures: a positive narrative ("profitable") with a built-in deferral ("until losses are cleared") that indefinitely delays any return to investors. Bath Attractions Ltd had 138 shareholders who invested at £1 per share; the shares are now being sold at 20p — an 80% discount.

⚑ Key Revelation 06 · Deflection to Phone Call

When the prospective investor pushes for written answers to due diligence questions, Leon repeatedly deflects to a phone call: "it will be easier to explain on a call." The prospective investor explicitly refuses: "I prefer matters pertaining to Due Diligence are in writing, not through a phone call." This is the same deflection pattern documented across the network — verbal assurances cannot be held to account; written responses can.

Reconstructed Exchange — Adam Leon · Prospective Investor · June 3–5, 2025

LEON →

Opens with a call request to explain a "brokered distressed sale" of HOF shares at 80% discount. No written details offered upfront. No mention of the bankruptcy or share transfer context.

KARYN →

Refuses phone call. Requests written financials and documentation to understand the discount before any engagement.

LEON →

Provides micro accounts. States 2,236,897 shares in issue at £1/share. Confirms 100,000 shares for distressed sale at 20p (40,000 already sold, 60,000 remaining). Claims HOF is "currently trading profitably."

KARYN →

Checks Companies House independently. Identifies a recent share transfer from founders to a named creditor. Asks why founders transferred shares at no cost and why that party is selling so quickly. Requests to speak to them directly.

LEON →

Again deflects to phone call. Confirms share transfer happened in January 2025. Says he can "put you in touch with whoever you want to speak to." Still no mention of bankruptcy.

KARYN →

Due diligence team raises the bankruptcy flag directly. States in writing that one of the founders was declared bankrupt in January 2025, coinciding with the share transfer. Warns of legal consequences for buyer, seller, and anyone aiding the transaction with knowledge of this. Asks for "the full picture."

LEON →

Confirms the lead contractor was not fully paid, and accepted shares from Posterity as consideration "before the personal bankruptcy." States: "This was done under advisement." Claims: "I am merely the messenger and had no involvement in any of these transactions."

⚑ Overall Significance of the Prospective Investor Exchange

This exchange is significant for five reasons. First, it confirms Adam Leon remains operationally active in JW-connected transactions more than two years after his stated resignation. Second, it confirms 750,000 founder shares in Bath Attractions Ltd were transferred to a creditor in January 2025, coinciding with a personal bankruptcy filing — a potential antecedent transaction under UK insolvency law. Third, it shows the same playbook in live operation: distressed sale framing, deflection to phone calls, partial disclosure, "currently profitable" narrative. Fourth, the phrase "done under advisement" implies a third party — almost certainly a solicitor or insolvency advisor — was aware of and guided this transfer. Fifth, The prospective investor's due diligence team independently identified the legal risk without any briefing from the activist group — indicating the concerns are not limited to existing investors but are visible to any competent professional conducting standard checks. This exchange has been forwarded to the investigation and is available for submission to TMP and the Insolvency Service.

Section 12 · Live Evidence · December 2025

"Peter Parfait"
The Playbook, Confirmed

In December 2025 an activist group member used the Wayback Machine to recover the archived website peterparfait.com — a property investment newsletter site active from approximately 2008 to 2011. The discovery was immediately described within the group as "BINGO!! JACKPOT" when the design language, fonts, and layout were identified as identical to Posterity Entertainment publications from 2019 onwards. A long-term acquaintance independently confirmed the alias without prompting. The two corroborations together establish a continuous playbook spanning at least 17 years: build a mailing list, create urgency around a compelling investment narrative, collect funds, and move on.

The Peter Parfait Playbook (c.2008–2011)

1. Build a mailing list — Newsletter subscribers sign up for free "insider" property investment tips. Trust is built over months of free content before any investment ask is made.
2. Position as exclusive access — Opportunities are framed as unavailable to the general public: auctions below market value, off-market deals, insider knowledge. Subscribers feel privileged, not solicited.
3. Create urgency with limited availability — "Only a few opportunities remain." Deadlines are stated. The closing of a round or an auction date provides natural pressure.
4. Use a persona, not a company — "Peter Parfait" is a named individual with a personality and a story. Personal brands build emotional connection faster than corporate ones. They are also harder to trace when things go wrong.

The Same Playbook · 2019–2025

1. Build a mailing list — Posterity Entertainment newsletter subscribers recruited from prior JW initiatives. The Ideas Factory (£800k shareholder funds before dissolving) was itself a subscriber-funded newsletter vehicle.
2. Position as exclusive access — SEIS tax relief framed as reducing investor risk: the income tax rebate lowers the apparent net cost of entry, while the practical difficulty of exiting within three years without triggering clawback creates a committed investor base once funds are in.
3. Create urgency — Closing rounds, "only a few memberships held back," double shares for late investors (described by investors as "outrageous"). Dubai club memberships sold the same day the club closed.
4. Use a persona — "Maria" replaces Adam Leon. Never photographed, never on a video call. A name that is "easier to buy from." Same function as Peter Parfait — human warmth without accountability.

"BINGO!! JACKPOT!!! YES, A definite link established. Well done on Wayback Machine. He and Polly were selling Llandrinio Hall when I knew them at Blacks. I remember looking at the details on Savills."

— [Name Removed], activist group correspondence, 11 December 2025

"The name Peter Parfait was all over it from 2008–11 and he even had his own website www.peterparfait.com. Note the website defaults to the ideasfactory.com — which lists lots of success stories, holiday accommodation etc. The site has all his hallmarks — the same look and feel, even the same font — of other Posterity publicity pages."

— Investor, activist group correspondence, 11 December 2025

"Would that fictitious name be Peter Parfait, the name he used as editor of property newsletters? That's the name he used when I first knew of him. He continued to use it for quite some time, and maybe still does."

— Long-term acquaintance, activist group correspondence, 8 December 2025 (identity withheld)

"I understand he is trying to raise money again….."

— [Name Removed] (source with direct knowledge of subject's movements), 8 December 2025

⚑ Investigator's note — active fundraising March 2026

As of March 2026, the subject's LinkedIn profile describes him as an "RWA Tokenisation Consultant, Originator." Sources report he is allegedly living in Wells, Somerset, under a fictitious name and continues to seek investment. The playbook documented above appears fully operational: four separate investment pitches have been circulated from p@peterparfait.com — covering property tokenisation, a survivalist estate, a global wellness token, and a film financing opportunity explicitly described as "zero risk." The last of these raises a specific additional regulatory question: if the film financing pitch constitutes a regulated financial promotion, the use of "zero risk" language would engage COBS 4.2, under which the FCA prohibits describing an investment as risk-free. Whether it qualifies is for the FCA to determine. These pitches are documented in full in Section 13 · Live Pitches. Any individual approached by the subject or by "Maria" with an investment opportunity is urged to conduct thorough due diligence and to report any approach to Report Fraud (reportfraud.police.uk) — which replaced Action Fraud in January 2026 (see gov.uk/government/news/action-fraud-replaced-by-new-national-fraud-reporting-service) — and the Insolvency Service (reference CC-2023-115641).

Section 13 · Live Evidence · March 2026

Four New Pitches
Same Address. Same Pattern.

While PMCC Ltd remains in liquidation, the Insolvency Service investigation continues, and investors await any return of funds, the p@peterparfait.com email address has been active. Four distinct investment pitches have been circulated to the same mailing list — each signed "Peter Parfait", each following the four-step playbook documented in Section 12, and each targeting a different theme: property tokenisation, a survivalist countryside estate, a global wellness token, and a film financing opportunity explicitly described as "zero risk."

Taken individually, each pitch might appear as a separate venture. Taken together — same sender, same call to action, same email address, sent while prior investors remain unpaid — they represent the continuation of a pattern this investigation has documented across 25 years: multiple simultaneous propositions, each appealing to a different investor psychology, circulated before any prior proposition has been accounted for. The fourth pitch raises a specific additional regulatory question: if this communication constitutes a financial promotion under s.21 FSMA 2000, the use of "zero risk" language would engage COBS 4.2 — under which the FCA specifically prohibits describing an investment as risk-free. Whether the communication qualifies as a regulated financial promotion is a matter for the FCA to determine.

Pitch 01 · Property Tokenisation

RWA Tokenisation Platform
Unlock your property without selling

Signed "Peter Parfait." Pitches a Real World Asset (RWA) tokenisation platform focused on property. Property owners are invited to tokenise their assets — including their own home — to "unlock value" without a traditional sale. Investors are invited as "partners." Contact: p@peterparfait.com.

Playbook elements present

✦ "We are still early. That matters." — urgency framing
✦ Global investor pool — exclusivity narrative
✦ "I've been involved in many property fundraising projects" — authority claim
✦ No company name, no registration, no prospectus
✦ Subject's LinkedIn describes him as "RWA Tokenisation Consultant"

Pitch 02 · Survivalist Estate

Semi-Off-Grid Homestead
South West England · Max 20 participants

Signed "Peter Parfait." Appeals to fears about AI, AGI, war, climate crisis, and global economic collapse. A "friend" is assembling a group of up to 20 households to acquire a countryside estate. Willis states he is "buying in myself." Property described as already identified, offer accepted. No address given. Contact: p@peterparfait.com.

Playbook elements present

✦ "Act before it's too late" — extreme urgency / fear framing
✦ "No more than 20 in total" — artificial scarcity
✦ "I am buying in myself" — trust signal with no verification
✦ "A friend of mine" — distancing / deniability
✦ No company, no legal structure, no address, no prospectus

Pitch 03 · Wellness Token

Global Wellness Sanctuary Initiative
Luxury · Longevity · AI · Token

Signed "Peter Parfait." Pitches a global network of luxury wellness sanctuaries across Spain, Portugal, Morocco, Costa Rica, Thailand, Bali and the USA. Powered by a proprietary "wellness utility token." Pre-launch token access offered at a discount. No company registered. No token listed. Contact: p@peterparfait.com.

Playbook elements present

✦ "Pre-launch access … discounted entry point" — urgency + exclusivity
✦ Seven global locations — grand scale with no evidence
✦ AI, longevity, biohacking — timely theme pivoting
✦ Crypto token with no exchange, no whitepaper, no registration
✦ No company name, no directors, no prospectus

⚑ Regulatory red flag

Pitch 04 · Film Financing

Zero Risk Film Financing
"reduce that risk to zero"

Signed "Peter Parfait." Pitches a film investment structure described as reducing financial exposure "to zero" — with "the first dollar through the box office" representing profit, and "guaranteed global distribution before a single frame is shot." Targets investors, people wanting to appear in films themselves or with their children, producers, entrepreneurs, and filmmakers. Sent from p@peterparfait.com. No company named. No structure described. No evidence of any distribution agreement.

Playbook elements present

✦ "zero risk" / "zero risk opportunity" — stated twice, explicitly
✦ "guaranteed global distribution" — unverifiable absolute claim
✦ "the holy grail of filmmaking" — grandiosity framing
✦ "only very recently become possible" — manufactured urgency
✦ "There's never been a better time" — standard closer
✦ Offering to let investors or their children appear in films — identical to ONIB recruitment
✦ No company, no structure disclosed, no prospectus, no FCA authorisation

⚑ Pattern Analysis — Four Pitches, One Mailing List

Each of the four pitches targets a different investor psychology: the property owner seeking liquidity; the anxious individual seeking safety; the wellness-conscious investor seeking purpose and returns; and the film investor being told risk has been eliminated. The themes are unrelated to each other. But the structure — the persona, the email address, the call to action, the absence of any legal or regulatory documentation — is identical across all four.

This is not a business developing a coherent strategy. It is a mailing list being worked across multiple narratives simultaneously, to see which one resonates. The fourth pitch raises a specific additional regulatory question not present in the others.

Common to all four

Signed "Peter Parfait" · Same email address · No registered company · No prospectus · No FCA authorisation · No verifiable address · Sent while liquidation ongoing

⚑ "Zero risk" — a specific regulatory prohibition

Whether this pitch constitutes a financial promotion depends on whether the underlying investment structure involves regulated investment activity under FSMA 2000 — the email does not disclose the structure. If it does qualify as a regulated financial promotion, then describing the investment as "zero risk" would engage COBS 4.2, under which the FCA specifically prohibits claims that an investment is risk-free, regardless of the underlying structure. This would apply independently of — and in addition to — the s.21 FSMA question of whether the promotion is authorised at all. Whether the communication qualifies, and whether any exemption applies, is a matter for the FCA to determine. What can be said without qualification: no investment carries zero risk, and no distribution can be guaranteed before production begins. Recipients of this pitch should treat "zero risk" language as a recognised warning sign and report it to the FCA at fca.org.uk/consumers/report-scam-unauthorised-firm.

s.21 FSMA 2000 — all four pitches

Under s.21 FSMA 2000, communicating an invitation or inducement to engage in investment activity in the course of business requires either FCA authorisation or an applicable exemption. None of these pitches carry any FCA registration, risk warning, or exemption statement. Whether each email constitutes a regulated financial promotion is a matter for the FCA to determine. Recipients can report at fca.org.uk/consumers/report-scam-unauthorised-firm.

"This is absolutely going back full circle to my roots, when I launched Property Auction News in 1996. Crikey, thirty years ago! I very much know how this works and the sector needs quite a bit of modernisation and disruption. The timing could not be better. And I have all the players in hand."

— "Peter Parfait," London Auction House pitch, March 2026

Note: "Property Auction News" is the newsletter this investigation has previously identified as part of the "Peter Parfait" alias operation, active c.2003–2011. The subject's claim to have "launched" it "in 1996" extends the documented timeline further than previously established. The self-referential nostalgia — "going back to my roots," "I very much know how this works" — mirrors the language used to establish authority in prior investment solicitations across 25 years.

Section 14

Source
Documents

Insolvency Service · Complaint

CC-2023-115641 — PMCC Ltd (12188280)

Filed November 2023. Five specific allegations covering misrepresentation at point of investment, fund misuse, communication failure, co-director irregularity, and failure to inform shareholders of insolvency. Confirmed ongoing as of March 2025.

Nov 2023Ongoing · TMP
INS

Subject Email · Oct 2023

"I am so, so sorry" — First True Financial Disclosure

Email 16 Oct 2023 from [email redacted]. First disclosure of the true position: £10k revenue vs £20k/week costs, £270k+ inherited debt, sixteen months of losses. Directly contradicts statements made at point of investment.

16 Oct 2023Authenticated
EML

Companies House · Live Data · March 2026

31 Companies — Both Officer IDs Cross-Referenced

Full corporate register verified directly from CH live register. Two officer IDs identified, combined. Key corrections: MASI formerly Marches Architectural Salvage; Fistful of Lead formerly Attack of the Sharks / Maleficent Seven; PMCC registered address now 29 Craven Street (liquidator). Bladud Spring and JoJopa confirmed fully compliant.

Verified Mar 2026CH Live
CH

Liquidator Docs · TMP

£116,000 Director Loan Account Written Off

TMP liquidation documents show £116k written off via the director's loan account. The director has verbally confirmed to the liquidator that this represents his salary — yet it is recorded in filed accounts as a loan. The discrepancy between verbal admission and official filing is a central concern of this investigation.

TMP LiquidationCritical
TMP

Investor Testimony · Compiled Evidence

Pooled Accounts — More Than a Dozen Investors

Compiled testimony and correspondence from more than a dozen investors across the activist group. Contains: 31-company corporate register analysis, 5 bank accounts, investor timeline, concerns register with direct testimony, shareholder stories, and bank account records submitted to liquidators TMP.

May 2024 (updated)Submitted to TMP
XLS

Written Confirmation · PDIC

Posterity Digital Investment Club Never Launched

Written confirmation from both Adam Leon and the director's wife that PDIC was never launched despite funds collected Oct 2021. Basis of direct fraud claim. Funds confirmed used elsewhere.

Confirmed in writingFraud claim basis

Archived Web · Wayback Machine

peterparfait.com — Design Language Match

Archived pages recovered Dec 2025. Identical font and layout to Posterity Entertainment publications. Independently corroborated by long-term acquaintance with 15+ year history with subject. Also links to ideasfactory.com investment newsletter infrastructure.

c.2008–2011 / Dec 2025Corroborated

LinkedIn · Public Profile

"RWA Tokenisation Consultant, Originator"

Subject's LinkedIn as of 2025 describes him as an RWA (Real World Asset) tokenisation consultant — consistent with reports of new investment solicitation while the PMCC liquidation remains unresolved. Consistent with step one of a new investment cycle.

Active · 2025Public Profile
LKN

Jonathan Willis: The Fall of Blacks Club, the Crypto Millions & a 25-Year Trail of Broken Promises

This report is compiled from UK Companies House public records (company numbers as cited, statuses verified live March 2026), Insolvency Service complaint CC-2023-115641, pooled investor testimony and correspondence, and contemporaneous correspondence supplied by shareholders. All assertions from investor sources represent the beliefs of those authors. Jonathan Willis has not been charged with any criminal offence. No identifying names of investors have been included. This report does not constitute legal advice.
